title: The Cold Weather Payment explained
description: How the Cold Weather Payment works: the £25 trigger, who qualifies, the postcode checker, and how it differs from Winter Fuel Payment and Warm Home Discount.
blurb: Who gets the automatic £25 when temperatures hit freezing for a week, how the trigger works, and what to do if yours is missing.
lede: The Cold Weather Payment is the most weather-dependent piece of help in the UK benefits system. When the average temperature in your area is recorded, or forecast, to be zero degrees or below for seven days in a row, an extra £25 lands in the bank account of every eligible household covered by that weather station. There is no form, no phone call and no application. In a mild winter you might get nothing at all; in a hard one, households in the coldest spots have received several payments in a single season. Because the whole thing runs silently in the background, plenty of people who qualify have no idea the scheme exists, and others wrongly assume it is the same thing as the Winter Fuel Payment. This guide covers how the trigger works, who qualifies, what £25 actually buys at current energy prices, and how to chase a payment you think you have missed.
short_answer: The Cold Weather Payment is £25 for each seven-day period between 1 November and 31 March in which the average temperature at your local weather station is recorded or forecast at 0°C or below. It is paid automatically, usually within 14 working days, to people on Pension Credit and certain other means-tested benefits in England, Wales and Northern Ireland. Scotland runs a different scheme, the Winter Heating Payment, which pays a fixed amount each winter regardless of the weather.

sections.html:
The scheme runs from 1 November to 31 March each winter. The Met Office monitors a network of weather stations across England, Wales and Northern Ireland, and every postcode area is mapped to one of them. A payment is triggered when the average of the daily mean temperatures at your station, over seven consecutive days, is zero degrees Celsius or below.

Two details matter here. First, it is the average over the full week that counts, not a single frosty night. A run of cold nights with milder afternoons can leave the weekly average above zero, which is why a winter can feel bitter without producing a single payment. Second, a forecast trigger counts as well as a recorded one. If the Met Office forecasts a qualifying week of freezing weather for your station, the payment is made even if the cold snap turns out milder than predicted, and you do not have to give it back.

Each separate seven-day trigger earns a separate £25. In a prolonged freeze, two or three back-to-back triggers at the same station are possible, so a household could receive £50 or £75 across a single cold spell. The money goes into the same account as your normal benefit payment, normally within 14 working days of each trigger.

Because everything hangs on which weather station your postcode is tied to, two qualifying households a few miles apart can have different outcomes in the same winter. Stations at higher altitude or further from the coast trigger far more often than urban or coastal ones.

The Cold Weather Payment is tied to means-tested benefits, not to age or to how cold your home is. Broadly, you may get it if you receive one of the following:

  • Pension Credit. Almost everyone on Pension Credit qualifies automatically. This is the simplest route in, and it is also the gateway to other winter help, so if you are of pension age and on a low income it is worth checking your entitlement.
  • Universal Credit. You usually qualify if you and your partner are not in work and your claim includes a disability element, limited capability for work, or a child under five living with you. If you have a disabled child on your claim, you can qualify even if you are working.
  • Income Support, income-based Jobseeker's Allowance or income-related Employment and Support Allowance. You generally need a disability or pensioner premium, a child under five, or a disabled child on the claim. People in the ESA support group qualify without a premium.
  • Support for Mortgage Interest. Recipients can qualify subject to similar household conditions.

The exact conditions are fiddly and they do change, so treat the list above as the shape of the scheme rather than the fine print. Check the current eligibility rules on the official GOV.UK Cold Weather Payment page before relying on a payment arriving.

If you qualify, there is nothing to do. The Department for Work and Pensions matches benefit records against triggered weather stations and pays automatically. The payment is tax-free, and it does not reduce any of your other benefits.

sections.h2: What £25 actually buys at current prices
sections.html:
The payment has been £25 per trigger since 2008, so its real value has been shrinking as energy prices have risen. Even so, in a genuinely freezing week it covers a meaningful slice of the extra heating you burn. Here is what £25 translates to at the current price cap, ignoring standing charges, which you pay every day regardless of how cold it is:

What £25 coversHow muchWorking
Gas (heating and hot water)About 341 kWh£25 ÷ 7.33p per kWh
ElectricityAbout 95 kWh£25 ÷ 26.11p per kWh
Gas central heating, typical 3-bed semiRoughly 20 to 25 hours of boiler run timeBoiler drawing about 15 kW of gas costs around £1.10 per hour
2 kW electric fan heaterAbout 47 hours2 kW × 26.11p = 52p per hour
Electric blanket (100 W)Around 950 hours0.1 kW × 26.11p = 2.6p per hour

At 26.11p per kWh electricity and 7.33p per kWh gas, Ofgem price cap July to September 2026. Boiler figure assumes a mid-size system; see our guide to the cost of central heating per hour for your own numbers.

The comparison makes one practical point clear. If you receive a Cold Weather Payment and need to stretch it, gas heating and low-wattage options like an electric blanket turn £25 into days or weeks of warmth, while a plug-in electric heater burns through it in two days of constant use.

sections.h2: Scotland and Northern Ireland do it differently
sections.html:
The Cold Weather Payment covers England and Wales, and Northern Ireland runs a parallel scheme with the same £25 trigger through its own Department for Communities. Scotland left the scheme entirely.

Since winter 2022-23, eligible Scottish households get the Winter Heating Payment instead. It is a single fixed payment made automatically between December and February to people on broadly the same qualifying benefits, and crucially it is paid regardless of the weather. For winter 2025-26 it was £59.75. The trade-off is plain: Scottish households get certainty every year, while households elsewhere can receive nothing in a mild winter or £75 or more in a severe one. Scotland also pays a Pension Age Winter Heating Payment in place of the UK Winter Fuel Payment.

If you have moved between Scotland and the rest of the UK, your entitlement follows your address on the qualifying date, so check the rules for the nation you actually live in.

sections.h2: How it fits alongside other winter help
sections.html:
The Cold Weather Payment is one of three winter schemes that people regularly mix up, and they stack. Receiving one does not stop you receiving the others.

  • Cold Weather Payment. £25 per freezing week, weather-dependent, paid to people on qualifying means-tested benefits. Could be £0 one winter and £75 the next.
  • Winter Fuel Payment. An annual payment for pensioner households, paid once each winter whatever the weather. The amount and the income rules have changed several times in recent years, so see our separate guide to the Winter Fuel Payment for the current position.
  • Warm Home Discount. Not a cash payment at all, but a one-off credit applied to your electricity account each winter. Our Warm Home Discount guide covers who gets it automatically.

A household on Pension Credit in a cold part of England could plausibly receive all three in the same winter. None of them shows up as a line you need to decode on your energy bill, with one exception: the Warm Home Discount appears as a credit on your electricity account, while the other two arrive as bank payments alongside your benefits.

If you are struggling with energy costs and do not qualify for any of these, there is still help available through supplier hardship funds and council schemes; see our guide on what to do if you cannot afford your energy bill.

sections.h2: Checking a trigger and chasing a missing payment
sections.html:
During the scheme window, GOV.UK runs a Cold Weather Payment postcode checker. Enter the first half of your postcode and it tells you whether your weather station has triggered this winter and how many times. The checker only operates from November to March; outside those months the page simply tells you the scheme is closed.

If the checker shows a trigger for your area, you believe you qualify, and nothing has arrived after 14 working days, the route depends on which benefit you receive:

  • Pension Credit recipients contact the Pension Service.
  • Universal Credit recipients report it through their online journal.
  • Income Support, JSA and ESA recipients contact Jobcentre Plus.

Common reasons a payment does not arrive are a change of address that has not reached the DWP, a benefit claim that started after the qualifying week, or household circumstances that fall just outside the conditions, such as a Universal Credit claimant with earnings in the assessment period. It is always worth asking rather than assuming, because the payment is an entitlement, not a discretionary grant.

The Cold Weather Payment is a small, sharp piece of targeted help: £25 per genuinely freezing week, paid automatically, with nothing to claim and no effect on your other benefits. Its weakness is the lottery element. The trigger depends on your local weather station, and a winter of cold nights and mild days can produce nothing at all, which is exactly why Scotland swapped it for a flat annual payment.

Two practical takeaways. First, if you are of pension age on a low income, claiming Pension Credit is the single move that opens the door to this payment and most other winter support at once. Second, because the payment only ever covers part of a cold snap's extra cost, the lasting win is cutting the amount of heat your home loses in the first place. Free or cheap measures like draught-proofing and topped-up loft insulation reduce every freezing week's cost, not just the ones that trigger a payment. Scheme rules and amounts change, so confirm the current details on the official GOV.UK Cold Weather Payment page each winter.

faq.q: Do I need to apply for the Cold Weather Payment?
faq.a: No. If you receive a qualifying benefit and your local weather station records or forecasts an average of 0°C or below over seven consecutive days between 1 November and 31 March, the £25 is paid automatically into the same account as your benefit, usually within 14 working days.

faq.q: How do I check whether my area has had a Cold Weather Payment trigger?
faq.a: Use the postcode checker on the GOV.UK Cold Weather Payment page during the scheme window, November to March. Enter the first part of your postcode and it shows whether your weather station has triggered this winter and how many times.

faq.q: Does the Cold Weather Payment affect my other benefits or get taxed?
faq.a: No. The payment is tax-free and it does not reduce Universal Credit, Pension Credit or any other benefit. You can also receive it in the same winter as the Winter Fuel Payment and the Warm Home Discount.

faq.q: Why did I not get a Cold Weather Payment in Scotland?
faq.a: Scotland left the scheme and replaced it with the Winter Heating Payment, a single fixed payment made automatically between December and February regardless of the weather. For winter 2025-26 it was £59.75.

faq.q: Is the Cold Weather Payment the same as the Winter Fuel Payment?
faq.a: No. The Winter Fuel Payment is an annual payment for pensioner households paid whatever the weather. The Cold Weather Payment is £25 per seven-day freezing spell and depends on means-tested benefits and your local temperature. Eligible households can receive both.
